heroOverview: Dracunculiasis — commonly called guinea worm disease — is caused by the parasitic nematode Dracunculus medinensis, acquired by drinking water containing infected copepod crustaceans. A female worm, which can grow up to 100 cm long, migrates subcutaneously over roughly a year before emerging through a painful blister, typically on the lower leg. Global eradication efforts led by the Carter Center and WHO have reduced annual cases from an estimated 3.5 million in the mid-1980s to single digits in recent years, making it the infectious disease closest to being eradicated without a vaccine or curative drug. There is no effective drug treatment; management consists solely of slow mechanical winding of the worm and prevention of secondary infection.

keyStats.prevalence: Fewer than 14 cases reported worldwide in 2023 (WHO); down from ~3.5 million cases annually in the 1980s
keyStats.demographics: Affects adults and children in rural communities dependent on surface water in sub-Saharan Africa; Chad accounts for the majority of recent human cases
keyStats.avgAge: All ages affected; agricultural workers and herders with occupational exposure to surface water at highest risk
keyStats.globalCases: 13 human cases confirmed globally in 2023 (WHO); near-eradication but canine reservoir in Chad complicates final elimination

definition: Dracunculiasis (ICD-10: B72) is a tissue-invasive nematode infection caused exclusively by Dracunculus medinensis, commonly known as the guinea worm or Medina worm. Humans acquire the infection by drinking unfiltered water containing Cyclops copepods that harbour infective third-stage larvae. After ingestion, larvae penetrate the gut wall, mature in retroperitoneal tissue over approximately one year, and mate; the male dies and the gravid female — which can reach 60-100 cm in length — then migrates toward the skin surface. When the worm is ready to expel larvae, it induces a blister, most commonly on the lower extremity, through which it slowly emerges. Contact of the emerging worm with water stimulates release of up to 3 million larvae, completing the life cycle. The infection falls under the specialist remit of infectious disease physicians and, in its complicated musculoskeletal forms, orthopaedic surgeons. It is classified as a neglected tropical disease targeted for eradication by the WHO; an animal reservoir in dogs and other canids in Chad has been identified as a complicating factor in the final elimination phase.

typesClassification.description: The most common presentation, in which the female worm emerges through the foot, ankle, or lower leg, causing a painful blister and subcutaneous track.
Worm emergence through the arm, hand, or trunk; occurs in about 10-15% of cases and carries similar risks of secondary infection and joint involvement.
Cases in which the worm breaks during extraction, enters a joint, or becomes secondarily infected with bacteria, causing septic arthritis, abscess, or osteomyelitis.
Rare cases in which worms migrate to unusual sites including the central nervous system, spinal cord, or pericardium, with potentially serious consequences.

primarySymptoms: A burning, intensely painful blister, typically 2-7 cm across, most often on the foot, ankle, or lower leg, appearing about one year after ingestion and heralding worm emergence.
The tip of the white worm visible in or protruding from the blister opening, which is pathognomonic for the disease.
Intense local pain and swelling around the blister site, often severe enough to incapacitate the patient for weeks.
Systemic allergic or urticarial reaction — generalised urticaria, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhoea — in the days before blister formation as the worm reaches the skin.
Fever and malaise coinciding with blistering and worm emergence, driven by the inflammatory response to worm products.
Pruritus over the affected limb for several days before blister eruption, a useful early warning sign.
Limitation of movement and weight-bearing disability when the worm is near or in a joint, or when secondary infection causes swelling.
Visible subcutaneous cord along the worm's track in the dermis and subcutaneous tissue, sometimes detectable by careful palpation before emergence.
Secondary bacterial infection signs — purulent discharge, increasing redness, warmth, and swelling — when the blister or extraction site becomes infected.
Repeated seasonal incapacitation in endemic communities, with peak transmission correlating to dry-season dependence on ponds.
earlyWarningSigns: A burning or itching patch on the lower leg appearing approximately 12 months after drinking unfiltered surface water in an endemic region.
A local swelling or tight sensation under the skin without a clear cause in an endemic area.
Urticaria, nausea, or mild fever developing suddenly before any blister is visible, suggesting the worm is breaching the dermis.
emergencySigns: Signs of septic arthritis — severe joint swelling, restricted movement, and high fever — indicating the worm has entered the joint space.
Deep spreading cellulitis or abscess formation at the extraction site, which can lead to septicaemia if untreated.
Neurological symptoms if the worm migrates aberrantly to the spinal cord or brain.
Tetanus if the wound becomes contaminated — tetanus immunisation should be verified in all patients.

causes.cause: Drinking water containing Cyclops copepods carrying infective larvae
causes.description: The only known transmission route. Cyclops crustaceans ingest free-swimming first-stage larvae released into water by emerging worms and develop them to the infective third stage. Humans who drink unfiltered stagnant water — particularly from ponds or step wells — swallow infected copepods; gastric acid kills the copepod but the larvae penetrate the gut wall.

causes.cause: Larval maturation in retroperitoneal tissue over approximately 12 months
causes.description: After penetrating the gut, male and female larvae mature and mate; the male worm dies. The fertilised female migrates toward the subcutaneous tissue over the course of roughly 9-12 months. No symptoms occur during this long pre-patent period, making early diagnosis impossible without epidemiological context.

causes.cause: Female worm migration to the skin and blister induction
causes.description: The gravid female secretes prostaglandins and other mediators that drive blister formation at the skin surface, most often on the lower extremities, possibly related to dependent oedema and gravity-driven migration. Contact of the emerging worm with water stimulates rapid discharge of larvae from the uterus.

causes.cause: Canine and other animal reservoir hosts (Chad)
causes.description: Recent studies have identified dogs and other animals in Chad as significant reservoir hosts, with infected animals releasing larvae into water bodies. This animal reservoir complicates eradication by maintaining transmission cycles that do not depend on human cases.

causes.cause: Environmental contamination of shared water sources
causes.description: Communities dependent on shared step wells, ponds, or water holes in dry-season periods allow simultaneous multiple exposures; a single infected person bathing a wound in the water source can seed it with millions of larvae, infecting many community members.

riskFactors.description: The sole transmission route; use of pipe filters that exclude Cyclops copepods, or boiling water, completely prevents infection. Communities dependent on shared ponds or step wells are at highest risk.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in Chad, Ethiopia, Cameroon, or Central African Republic
riskFactors.description: These four countries account for virtually all remaining human cases. Chad alone reported the majority of cases in recent years. Hyperendemic transmission is linked to dry-season dependence on open water sources.

riskFactors.factor: Agricultural work and livestock herding in endemic areas
riskFactors.description: Occupational exposure to ponds and natural water bodies during irrigation or herding increases daily contact with potentially infected water sources.

riskFactors.factor: Absence of access to safe piped or treated water
riskFactors.description: In communities without safe water infrastructure, reliance on surface water for all daily needs creates unavoidable exposure; water infrastructure investment is the most sustainable long-term preventive strategy.

No protective immunity develops after infection; re-infection the following year is possible if water source use continues. Multiple sequential infections over years compound musculoskeletal complications.

riskFactors.factor: Contact with infected animals (dogs, cats) in or near water sources
riskFactors.description: In Chad, infected domestic dogs wading in shared water sources introduce larvae; community members using the same water for drinking are exposed to copepods that have fed on canine-sourced larvae.

affectedDemographics: Rural subsistence farmers and herders in sub-Saharan Africa, particularly in Chad, Ethiopia, Cameroon, and Central African Republic
Children who swim or play in endemic water sources
Women and girls in communities where water collection is a female responsibility
Communities without access to safe piped or treated drinking water
Domestic animals, particularly dogs, in Chad where animal transmission sustains the remaining cycle
diagnosisOverview: Dracunculiasis is one of the most clinically distinctive diagnoses in medicine: the visible emergence of a white worm from a lower-extremity blister is pathognomonic and requires no laboratory confirmation. In practice, the WHO case definition specifies a person presenting with a skin lesion or blister from which a worm is emerging or has emerged. Diagnosis is therefore primarily clinical and epidemiological — a patient in or recently returned from an endemic area with a characteristic lower-limb blister and visible worm needs no further testing to confirm the diagnosis. Blood tests are not diagnostic; eosinophilia may be present during the pre-patent period but is non-specific. Imaging — plain radiography, ultrasound, or MRI — is used in complicated or aberrant cases to locate a worm that has not emerged, to detect secondary joint involvement, or to identify deep-tissue abscess formation. Serological tests have been developed for research purposes but are not needed for clinical diagnosis. In the current near-eradication phase, the WHO global surveillance system requires that every possible case be reported immediately to national eradication programme coordinators for containment measures.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive diagnosis; the emerging white worm is directly visualised at the blister site, confirming dracunculiasis without further testing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician examines the blister; the worm tip is visible or can be induced to emerge by applying cold water. No pain is associated with examination itself, though the wound is very tender.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies a calcified worm in cases of worm death in situ, and detects secondary complications such as periosteal reaction, osteomyelitis, or joint destruction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard X-ray of the affected limb; quick, painless, minimal radiation dose.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Locates un-emerged or deep worms by the characteristic tubular echogenic appearance; also evaluates joint effusions and abscess collections.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A bedside gel-based scan; non-invasive and immediately available in most settings where it is likely to be needed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: May show eosinophilia during the pre-patent phase, supporting a diagnosis of tissue-invasive helminthiasis in an endemic-area traveller before worm emergence.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood draw; results within hours. Eosinophilia is non-specific but contextually helpful.

diagnosticTests.purpose: In suspected septic arthritis complicating worm migration into a joint, aspirated fluid reveals the infective agent or sterile eosinophilic effusion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Needle aspiration of joint fluid by a clinician under sterile conditions; sent urgently for microscopy and culture.

treatmentOverview: There is no curative drug for dracunculiasis — no antiparasitic agent kills Dracunculus medinensis effectively in vivo, and no vaccine exists. The only treatment is slow, careful mechanical extraction: the emerging worm is wound a few centimetres per day around a small stick or gauge, taking one to four weeks to extract a full-length adult female (up to 100 cm) without breaking it. Breaking the worm is a serious complication; the worm's contents are highly antigenic and their release into tissue causes severe inflammatory reactions, abscess formation, and risk of septicaemia. The wound is kept clean with sterile water, and topical antibiotics are applied to prevent secondary bacterial infection, which is the main cause of long-term disability in survivors. Oral antibiotics are given if secondary bacterial infection develops. Metronidazole and thiabendazole have been used historically but are not reliably active against the adult worm; their only benefit appears to be reducing perilesional inflammation rather than killing the parasite. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs can reduce local pain and swelling during extraction. Anti-tetanus prophylaxis should be confirmed or administered, as tetanus is a recognised lethal complication of wound contamination. Orthopaedic intervention — drainage of abscesses, arthroscopy, or joint debridement — is required in complicated cases with joint or bone involvement. From a public-health standpoint, containment during worm emergence — preventing the patient from entering any water source — is as much a part of case management as wound care, since it stops larvae from seeding shared water and perpetuating transmission.

medicalTreatments.name: Mechanical worm extraction
medicalTreatments.description: The standard and only curative intervention; the emerging worm is gently wound several centimetres per day around a small gauge or stick secured to the skin, proceeding cautiously over one to four weeks until the complete worm is removed. The wound is rinsed and dressed daily.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure achieved in virtually 100% of cases when the worm is fully extracted intact; complications arise in roughly 10-30% of cases from worm breakage or secondary infection.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical wound care and antiseptics
medicalTreatments.description: Daily cleaning with clean water and application of topical antibiotic ointment (e.g., bacitracin or mupirocin) to the extraction wound prevents secondary bacterial infection, the leading cause of lasting disability.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces secondary bacterial infection rates; exact quantification varies by setting, but proper wound care substantially lowers risk of abscess formation.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral antibiotics (e.g. amoxicillin-clavulanate or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole)
medicalTreatments.description: Used when secondary bacterial infection of the wound is established; choice guided by local antibiotic sensitivity patterns and wound culture where available.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolution of wound infection in approximately 85-95% of cases when appropriate antibiotics are given promptly.

medicalTreatments.name: Metronidazole
medicalTreatments.description: Historically given to reduce perilesional inflammation and facilitate extraction; does not kill the adult worm but may reduce tissue reaction, making extraction marginally easier. Use is now limited to cases with significant inflammation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: No evidence of worm-killing activity; anecdotal reports of approximately 20-30% improvement in extraction ease in retrospective series.

medicalTreatments.name: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
medicalTreatments.description: Given orally or topically to reduce pain and perilesional swelling during the extraction period, improving patient tolerance of the prolonged procedure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective analgesic and anti-inflammatory benefit; no effect on the parasite itself.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical extraction of unruptured or deep worm
surgicalOptions.description: When the worm cannot be safely extracted by mechanical winding — because it is deep, has coiled, or risks breakage — surgical exposure and intact removal under anaesthesia is performed.
surgicalOptions.successRate: High success rate when performed by an experienced surgeon; avoids the risk of worm breakage and associated inflammatory complications.

surgicalOptions.name: Abscess drainage and joint debridement
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical drainage of secondary abscesses or arthroscopic debridement of septic joints caused by a broken or infected worm; sometimes combined with wash-out and antibiotic irrigation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful resolution of acute septic complications in the majority of cases when performed promptly; long-term joint function depends on extent of prior damage.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pipe filter use for drinking water
alternativeTreatments.description: A fine-mesh pipe filter worn around the neck and used to filter all drinking water excludes infected Cyclops copepods and provides personal protection at near-100% efficacy when used consistently.

alternativeTreatments.name: Chemical larviciding of water sources (temephos)
alternativeTreatments.description: The organophosphate temephos applied monthly to endemic water bodies kills Cyclops copepods and reduces transmission; it is an approved WHO strategy for eradication campaigns and is safe at recommended doses.

whySeeSpecialist: Although mechanical extraction can be performed by trained community health workers, infectious disease specialist input is critical for complicated cases — those with secondary bacterial infection, suspected joint involvement, worm breakage, or aberrant migration. Orthopaedic surgical consultation is needed when the worm has entered a joint or bone. In endemic regions, national Guinea Worm Eradication Programme coordinators provide case support and surveillance resources; international travellers diagnosed outside endemic zones should be referred to a tropical medicine unit.
doctorSelectionGuide: For travellers or migrants diagnosed outside endemic regions, a tropical medicine or infectious disease unit at a university hospital is the best resource. These centres have experience with parasitic wound management and can access reference laboratory support for any serological testing needed. When returning to endemic areas for repeated exposure risks, work with community health workers trained in Guinea worm surveillance and containment to ensure proper wound care protocols are followed.
preventionStrategies: Filter all drinking water through a fine-mesh pipe filter or boil water from natural sources in endemic areas.
Apply WHO-approved temephos larvicide monthly to endemic step wells and ponds to kill Cyclops copepods.
Never enter a water source when a worm is emerging from a wound — this contaminates the water and perpetuates the infection cycle.
Treat all drinking-water sources in endemic villages with pipe-filter distribution programmes.
Prevent infected dogs and other animals from entering shared water sources in areas with known animal reservoir transmission.
Participate in national and community-level surveillance: immediately report any person with a visible worm emerging from the skin to programme coordinators.
lifestyleModifications: During worm emergence, remain isolated from all water bodies — do not swim, bathe in shared water, or wade in ponds — for the full duration of extraction.
Keep the wound site clean and covered with a sterile dressing between extraction sessions.
Accept full tetanus prophylaxis if vaccination status is uncertain or lapsed.
Plan economic and agricultural activities around the extraction period since incapacitation commonly lasts 2-4 weeks and can extend to months.
Enrol in community health monitoring if living in an endemic village, which allows early detection and containment of new cases.

recommended: Safe water from piped, boiled, or filter-treated sources exclusively
Adequate nutrition and protein intake to support wound healing during the prolonged extraction period
Oral rehydration solutions if fever and fluid loss occur during the early allergic phase
avoid: Drinking directly from ponds, step wells, or streams in endemic regions
Using unfiltered surface water for any oral intake — even brief sips during field work

exerciseGuidelines: Physical activity and weight-bearing should be minimised on the affected limb while the worm is being extracted; forcing movement risks worm breakage. Complete bed rest is not necessary but the patient should be supported to avoid stress on the wound site. After full extraction and wound healing — typically 4-8 weeks — normal physical activity can resume. Joint complications may require a longer recovery period with physiotherapy.
dailyManagement: Wind the worm 1-5 cm per session daily; do not force extraction — stop if resistance is felt.
Rinse the wound with clean water and apply fresh antiseptic dressing at each extraction session.
Take prescribed antibiotics for the full course if secondary infection is diagnosed.
Keep a record of worm length extracted and wound appearance for programme surveillance purposes.
Attend follow-up appointments to confirm complete extraction and screen for joint or bone complications.
prognosis: The prognosis for uncomplicated dracunculiasis is good: the worm eventually emerges and is removed, and the wound heals completely in the great majority of patients within four to eight weeks. However, secondary bacterial infection — which occurs in roughly 10-50% of cases in endemic settings — significantly worsens the outlook, causing deep abscesses, septic arthritis, osteomyelitis, and permanent joint damage that results in lasting disability. A substantial proportion of individuals in endemic communities suffer repeated annual infections over many years, accumulating musculoskeletal disability. Broken worms or those that migrate into joints carry the highest complication rates. With the near-eradication of the disease, the global burden of long-term disability is diminishing, but survivors of complicated disease in previous decades carry permanent physical impairment that affects agricultural productivity and quality of life.
recoveryTimeline: Uncomplicated worm extraction takes 1-4 weeks depending on the length of the worm and the rate of emergence. Wound healing after complete extraction typically occurs over a further 2-4 weeks. Secondary bacterial infections extend this timeline by weeks to months and may require hospitalisation. Joint complications can require 3-6 months of rehabilitation, with residual functional limitation in severe cases. After complete recovery without complications, affected individuals are fully mobile and have no residual systemic effects.
complications: Secondary bacterial infection — the most frequent and serious complication, occurring in 10-50% of cases; leads to abscess, cellulitis, and potential septicaemia.
Septic arthritis and osteomyelitis — when the worm enters a joint or bone, causing permanent joint destruction and disability in a significant proportion of affected limbs.
Worm rupture during extraction — releases antigenic worm contents into tissue, provoking severe inflammatory reaction, abscess formation, and risk of systemic sepsis.
Permanent physical disability — repeated infections and joint complications lead to contractures, arthritis, and functional impairment affecting mobility and the ability to work.
Tetanus — a recognised complication of wound contamination; preventable by prior immunisation.
Aberrant migration to the CNS, pericardium, or other unusual sites — extremely rare but potentially life-threatening.

confusedWithConditions.name: Loiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Loiasis (Loa loa) also produces subcutaneous worm migration and eye involvement, but the worm is much shorter (3-7 cm), emerges transiently across the conjunctiva, and is acquired from deerfly bites in West/Central Africa — not from drinking water.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gnathostomiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gnathostomiasis produces a similar migrating subcutaneous swelling from raw fish or reptile consumption, but the worm rarely emerges through the skin and requires histopathology or serology rather than direct worm visualisation to diagnose.

confusedWithConditions.name: Mansonelliasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Mansonella species are transmitted by insect bites and cause blood-detectable microfilariae with generally mild or absent cutaneous symptoms rather than the dramatic skin-emerging worm of dracunculiasis.

relatedConditions.name: Sparganosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are tissue-invasive helminthiases transmitted through water sources and cause subcutaneous worm migration; sparganosis involves Spirometra tapeworm larvae rather than a roundworm.

relatedConditions.name: Loiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Loiasis similarly produces a visible subcutaneous worm migrating through tissues, making it an important differential when a patient from sub-Saharan Africa presents with a moving swelling.

relatedConditions.name: Filariasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Lymphatic filariasis is another filarial worm infection endemic to tropical regions causing significant disability, sharing overlapping endemic zones and public-health eradication priorities.

relatedConditions.name: Onchocerciasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A related filarial infection transmitted in sub-Saharan Africa causing skin and eye disease, co-endemic with areas previously endemic for guinea worm.

faqs.question: What is guinea worm disease?
faqs.answer: Guinea worm disease (dracunculiasis) is a parasitic infection caused by the nematode Dracunculus medinensis, acquired by drinking water containing infected water fleas. A female worm up to 100 cm long grows inside the body for about a year, then slowly emerges through a painful blister on the skin.

faqs.question: How do you get guinea worm disease?
faqs.answer: You get guinea worm disease by drinking unfiltered water from ponds, step wells, or streams that contain tiny water fleas (Cyclops copepods) carrying infective worm larvae. It cannot be spread from person to person.

faqs.question: Is guinea worm disease eradicated?
faqs.answer: Not yet, but it is close. Cases fell from about 3.5 million in the 1980s to just 13 confirmed human cases in 2023, making it the most successfully controlled parasitic disease in history. A dog reservoir in Chad remains the main obstacle to final eradication.

faqs.question: What countries still have guinea worm disease?
faqs.answer: As of 2023, the remaining human cases are concentrated in Chad, with sporadic cases in Cameroon, Ethiopia, and the Central African Republic. Infected dogs are the main reservoir in Chad.

faqs.question: Is there a drug treatment or vaccine for guinea worm?
faqs.answer: No. There is no curative drug and no vaccine for guinea worm disease. The only treatment is slowly winding the worm out by hand over one to four weeks. Prevention relies entirely on water filtration and public-health measures.

faqs.question: How is guinea worm removed?
faqs.answer: The worm is slowly wound a few centimetres per day around a small stick, which is secured to the skin near the wound. The process takes one to four weeks. Breaking the worm is dangerous and must be avoided.

faqs.question: How long does guinea worm disease last?
faqs.answer: The incubation period is about 12 months from drinking infected water to worm emergence. Worm extraction takes 1 to 4 weeks, and wound healing takes a further 2 to 4 weeks in uncomplicated cases.

faqs.question: Why is guinea worm almost eradicated without a drug or vaccine?
faqs.answer: Eradication has been driven entirely by behaviour change — teaching communities to filter drinking water through pipe filters, treating water sources with safe larvicide, and containing cases by preventing patients from entering water. This shows how prevention alone can nearly eliminate a disease.

faqs.question: What happens if a guinea worm breaks during extraction?
faqs.answer: If the worm breaks, its contents leak into the tissue, causing a severe inflammatory reaction, painful swelling, and a high risk of secondary bacterial abscess. This is why extraction must be slow and never forced.

faqs.question: Can guinea worm disease cause permanent disability?
faqs.answer: Yes. Secondary bacterial infection, which occurs in 10 to 50 percent of cases, can cause septic arthritis, osteomyelitis, and lasting joint damage. People who suffer repeated annual infections over many years frequently develop permanent physical disability.

faqs.question: Does guinea worm disease affect children?
faqs.answer: Yes, children are affected equally to adults. Children who play in or drink from endemic water sources are at the same risk as adults, and the disease disrupts school attendance for weeks to months during worm emergence.

faqs.question: How do you prevent guinea worm disease?
faqs.answer: Prevention is through filtering all drinking water with a pipe filter, boiling water from natural sources, and applying WHO-approved temephos larvicide to endemic water bodies. Never enter a water source when a worm is emerging from your skin.

faqs.question: Why is guinea worm so painful?
faqs.answer: The pain comes from the worm secreting inflammatory mediators as it breaches the skin, causing a blister and intense local inflammation. Secondary bacterial infection of the wound makes the pain considerably worse.

faqs.question: Can guinea worm go to the brain?
faqs.answer: Aberrant migration to the central nervous system is extremely rare but has been reported. Most worms migrate to lower extremities. Brain involvement would cause neurological symptoms and is a medical emergency.

faqs.question: Is guinea worm the same as tapeworm?
faqs.answer: No. Guinea worm is a roundworm (nematode) of the species Dracunculus medinensis. Tapeworms are a separate class of parasites (cestodes). They are unrelated, though both can cause tissue-invasive disease.

faqs.question: What does the guinea worm look like?
faqs.answer: The adult female is a white, thread-like worm up to 100 cm long and about 1-2 mm in diameter — similar in appearance to a thick piece of string or spaghetti. The male is much shorter, about 1.2 cm.

faqs.question: What is the role of dogs in guinea worm transmission?
faqs.answer: In Chad, domestic dogs are infected with Dracunculus medinensis and release larvae into shared water sources when worms emerge through their paws. Community members who drink from the same water can then be infected, creating a transmission cycle that does not depend on human cases.

faqs.question: Can guinea worm disease recur?
faqs.answer: Yes. Prior infection provides no protective immunity. People in endemic communities can be reinfected each dry season if they continue to drink unfiltered water, and repeated infections are the main cause of cumulative disability.

faqs.question: Should travellers to Africa worry about guinea worm?
faqs.answer: The risk for travellers is extremely low given that only a handful of cases occur globally each year in remote rural areas. Travellers to endemic regions should avoid drinking unfiltered surface water, but this precaution applies to many other waterborne infections as well.

metaDescription: Guinea worm disease causes a worm up to 100 cm long to emerge through the skin after a year inside the body. Learn symptoms, extraction and prevention.
